coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of nanostructured zinc oxide and its composite with carbon dots for DSSCs applications using flexible electrode
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Publishing services by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of KeAi Communications Co. Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Publishing services by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of KeAi Communications Co. Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Etefa, Habtamu Fekadu
Dejene, Francis Birhanu
coredata.dc:description: ZnO nanowires (ZnO NWs), ZnO nanoparticles (ZnO NPs) and carbon dots (C-dots) were synthesized by hydrothermal, sol-gel and hydrothermal methods respectively. They were also characterized and applied for dye sensitized solar cells (DSSCs). The effects of C-dots on ZnO NWs and ZnO NPs have been evaluated. The C-dots were used at a mole ratio of citric acid (CA) to ethylene diamine (EDA) of 1 : 1.5. These C-dots were found to enhance the performance of the flexible electrode DSSCs. After the addition of C-dots, the power conversion efficiency (PCE) of ZnO NPs was boosted to be two times higher than that of ZnO NPs DSSCs without C-dots. Similarly, the ultraviolet (UV)-band revealed a blue shift, resulting in a lower band gap and a reduced charge transfer resistance, which can enhance the PCE of DSSCs. The loaded quantity on the flexible electrode substrate made of polyethylene terephthalate (PET) was optimized (50 mg). For DSSCs, the PET flexible electrode conductive polymer has produced positive outcomes. For ZnO NWs and ZnO NWs@C-dots, the PCE values were 1.45% and 4.25%; while for ZnO NPs and ZnO NPs@C-dots, they were 2.34% and 5.81%, respectively. This work achieved remarkable and competitive performance when compared to solid (indium tin oxides/glass)-based substrate.
coredata.openaccessSponsorName: Chongqing Xixin Tianyuan Data & Information Co., Ltd.
coredata.dcterms:subject: ZnO NWs@C-dots
ZnO NPs@C-dots
PET
DSSCs
Flexible electrode

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of ZnO NWs. (b, c) TEM image and size distribution of ZnO NPs.
(a) UV–visible absorption spectra and (b) PL emission and excitation spectra.
FT-IR transmittance spectra of ZnO NWs, ZnO NPs, C-dot and ZnO NPs@C-dot.
X-ray diffraction of ZnO NWs, ZnO NPs, ZnO NWs@C-dots and ZnO NPs@C-dots.
Nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms of ZnO NPs, ZnO NWs@C-dots and ZnO NPs@C-dots at 77 K.
Parameters obtained from electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) and Tauc's plots of ZnO NWs, ZnO NPs, ZnO NWs@C-dots, and ZnO NPs@C-dots.
(a) I–V curves of a ZnO NWs, b ZnO NPs, c ZnO NWs@C-dots and d ZnO NPs@C-dots. (b) Dye-adsorption vs band gap. (c) PCE (%) vs band gap.
FT-IR transmittance bands (at cm−1) and their assignments of ZnO NWs, ZnO NPs, C-dots and ZnO NPs@C-dot. (s: strong, m: medium, w: weak).
BET analysis of adsorption-desorption isotherms of specific surface area, pore diameter, and pore volume.
Parameters from J-V characteristics of ZnO NWs, ZnO NPs, ZnO NWs@C-dots and ZnO NPs@C-dots.
Comparison of PCE performance for DSSCs flexible electrodes made of various materials concerning commercial and uncommercial substrates.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of nanostructured zinc oxide and its composite with carbon dots for DSSCs applications using flexible electrode

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Walter Sisulu University, Private Bag X-1, Mthatha, 5117, South Africa
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Walter Sisulu University, Private Bag X-1, Mthatha, 5117, South Africa

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: ZnO nanowires (ZnO NWs), ZnO nanoparticles (ZnO NPs) and carbon dots (C-dots) were synthesized by hydrothermal, sol-gel and hydrothermal methods respectively. They were also characterized and applied for dye sensitized solar cells (DSSCs). The effects of C-dots on ZnO NWs and ZnO NPs have been evaluated. The C-dots were used at a mole ratio of citric acid (CA) to ethylene diamine (EDA) of 1 : 1.5. These C-dots were found to enhance the performance of the flexible electrode DSSCs. After the addition of C-dots, the power conversion efficiency (PCE) of ZnO NPs was boosted to be two times higher than that of ZnO NPs DSSCs without C-dots. Similarly, the ultraviolet (UV)-band revealed a blue shift, resulting in a lower band gap and a reduced charge transfer resistance, which can enhance the PCE of DSSCs. The loaded quantity on the flexible electrode substrate made of polyethylene terephthalate (PET) was optimized (50 mg). For DSSCs, the PET flexible electrode conductive polymer has produced positive outcomes. For ZnO NWs and ZnO NWs@C-dots, the PCE values were 1.45% and 4.25%; while for ZnO NPs and ZnO NPs@C-dots, they were 2.34% and 5.81%, respectively. This work achieved remarkable and competitive performance when compared to solid (indium tin oxides/glass)-based substrate.

ce:section.ce:para:
In comparison to conventional silicon-based devices, dye sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) are a promising eco-friendly, clean, efficient, and affordable photovoltaic (PV) device with an easy fabrication process. It also has the potential to be employed as flexible devices for walls or windows in building-integrated PV. 1 Dye and semiconductor oxides make up the majority of DSSCs. As an electron transport medium, many semiconductor oxides like SnO2, 2 TiO2, 3 ZnO, 4–6 and Nb2O5 have been employed. ZnO has been demonstrated effective performance and is a good alternative for DSSCs due to its unique properties, including ease of synthesis, non-toxicity, and low cost among others. 1
,
6

Due to its semiconductivity, high excitation binding energy (60 MeV), band gap of 3.37 eV, high stability in electrochemistry, non-toxicity, doping compatibility, and low cost, ZnO NPs 5
,
7 have attracted a lot of attention. 8 It is also widely utilized in photonics, solar cells, nanogenerators, photovoltaic devices, strain sensors, biosensors, chemical sensing devices, and photocatalysts because of its electrical and optical capabilities. 9 For improving semiconductor materials for nanoelectronic and photonic applications, 10 ZnO-NPs offer an intriguing alternative. Other significant applications include photocatalysts, sensors, dye-sensitized solar cells, etc. 11 Wide bandgap semiconductors like ZnO materials, which only absorb light in the UV spectrum and have a band gap of 3.1–3.3 eV. To increase ZnO's light absorption in the visible spectrum, it can also be linked with substances with smaller energy gaps, such as dye sensitizers, organic polymers, and semiconductors with reduced band gaps. 11 Because of its low cost, friendly conditions, precise process control, and extensive use in the industry, the electrochemical deposition approach is emerging as a significant method for the manufacture of ZnO nanowires (ZnO NWs). One of the most significant nanomaterials for nanotechnology in the current study is ZnO NW. 12 Many optoelectronic systems, including touch screens, organic light-emitting diodes, and solar cells, rely heavily on transparent electrodes. 13 Lightweight, thin, and bendable flexible solar cells based on plastic substrates are available. They are easily integrated into curved forms and may be made at a cheap cost, making them ideal for wearable and portable applications.

Carbon dots (C-dots) are an emerging class of luminescent carbon nanomaterials with promising applications in catalysis, drug delivery, bioimaging, biosensing, and energy storage. They have small sizes, excellent water solubility, low toxicity, tunable light-emitting properties, and good biocompatibility. Most materials belonging to the carbon family are made of fullerenes, 14 carbon nanotubes, diamonds, graphene, 15 and C-dots. 16 Recently, coated ITO has been applied to the most widely used plastic substrates polyethylene terephthalate (PET) and polyethylene naphthalate (PEN), which are employed as conductive polymer substrates in DSSCs. 17 However, for non-flexible substrates utilized in DSSCs, the anode and cathode films were heated for 30 min at 450 °C to remove residues and solvent present in the precursor. Fortunately, the thermal stability of PET and PEN polymer substrates depends on the maximum processing temperatures, which means that flexible electrode (ITO/PEN and ITO/PET substrates) starts to deform when their maximum temperature exceeds 150 °C. 18 Therefore, it suggested that the temperature treatment of polymer substrate should not exceed 150 °C. For this reason, such low-temperature treatment (low annealing) of polymer substrate resulted in poor performance in DSSC because the residues of the precursor cannot be effectively decomposed.

To increase the PCE of ZnO NWs and ZnO NPs in DSSC and evaluate the impact of C-dots on ZnO NWs and ZnO NPs DSSC, we created composites of ZnO NWs and ZnO NPs with C-dots in this work. Subsequently, composites of C-dots with ZnO NWs and ZnO NPs were formed under appropriate conditions to optimize the efficiency of composites for use in a DSSC, which was fabricated using di-tetrabutylammonium cis-bis-(isothiocyanato)bis(2,2′-bipyridyl-4,4′-dicarboxylato) ruthenium(II) (N719) dyes as sensitizers. Therefore, we anticipate the C-dots to function as a potential low-cost addition.

Sodium bromide (NaBr), sodium borohydride (NaBH4), zinc nitrate hexahydrate (98%), ethylenediamine (99%), ethylene glycol (EG), potassium iodide (KI), sodium carbonate (99.5%), and PET were brought from Acros Organics (USA). Acetonitrile, ruthenium 535 bisTBA (N719), sodium hydroxide (95%), and hydrochloric acid (HCI) were acquired from J.T. Baker in Taiwan, China, UniRegion Bio-Tech in Taiwan, China, MACRON Fine Chemicals in Sweden, and Scharlau in Spain, respectively. All of the solutions were made using ultra-pure water with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ cm.

An X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, D2 Phaser, Germany), an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, VG Scientific, ESCALAB 250, England), a Fourier transform infrared absorption spectrophotometer (FTIR, NICOLET 6700, Thermo Scientific, USA), a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL, Japan) were employed to characterize the prepared nanostructured ZnO materials. An ultraviolet (UV)/visible absorption spectrophotometry analysis on a photovoltaic cell, an electrochemical measurement using a Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) (BEL SORB Max, Japan) and a Zahner CIMPS-X photo electrochemical workstation in conjunction with the THALES software package (X pot-26366) for solar energy were carried out.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nanostructured ZnO (NWs, NPs) and C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: ZnO NWs were prepared by using hydrothermal techniques and the process outlined by Alshehri et al. 19 Firstly, 40 mL sodium carbonate (3.66 M) and 15 mL zinc nitrate hexahydrates (0.046 M) were mixed together for 10 min. The suspension was then poured into a 60 mL Teflon container and placed in an autoclave. The autoclave was heated for 6 h at 140 °C and cooled to room temperature. The whitish substance was obtained and then cleaned by filtering water until the pH reached 7. Overnight, the white products were allowed to dry at room temperature. Based on the process outlined by Efa et al., 20 ZnO NPs were prepared by using sol-gel techniques. 1 M solution of sodium hydroxide (NaOH) was added dropwise into the solution of zinc nitrate hexahydrate (1 M) in ethylene glycol (25 mL), and then the mixture was agitated at 80 °C for 1 h. The leftover ethylene glycol was then removed from the white suspension using a centrifuge with DI water and ethanol.

The hydrothermal process was also used to create carbon dots. 21 Citric acid (1 g) and ethylenediamine (0.3 mL) were combined with water (60 mL), stirred for 5 min, and then placed in an autoclave with a Teflon container. The autoclave was heated at 230 °C for 5 h, and after a complete reaction, it was cooled to room temperature. Following the evaporation of the solvent, the created C-dots were dried for 3 h at 100 °C. The carbon dots were obtained. To prepare ZnO NPs/C-dots, the appropriate ratio of C-dots was added to the fixed amount of ZnO NPs followed by stirring for 4 h. After the completed reaction, the product was washed by ethanol and DI water, respectively, and consequently kept in an oven at 100 °C for 3 h.

PEN flexible electrodes were cut into (1 × 1 cm2) pieces and pre-cleaned using acetone, ethanol and DI water. ZnO NWs, ZnO NPs, ZnO NWs@C-dots and ZnO NPs@C-dots (50 mg for each) dispersions in 200 μL of DI water were independently stirred for 4 h then coated using spin coating and annealed in an oven at 80 °C for 30 min. The aqueous dispersions were applied to a 1 × 1 cm2 section of the PET flexible electrode that had been previously cleaned and then allowed to dry. The coated electrode was submerged in an ethanol solution (5 mL) containing 0.5 mM N719 for 12 h after being annealed for 25 min at 80 °C. The platinum (Pt)/PET counter electrode was built with the mentioned samples on the PET electrode.

Separately, the annealed electrodes were submerged for 12 h in ethanol (5 mL) solutions of N719 (0.5 mM). The electrodes coated with ZnO NWs, ZnO NPs, ZnO NWs@C-dots, and ZnO NPs@C-dots have been immersed in a NaOH aqueous solution (5 mL) of 1 M for 12 h separately to extract dye loaded on the four samples electrodes after the additional dyes were removed using water. The calorimetric method was used to calculate the amounts of the dyes in the extractants using calibration curves.

PEN was pre-cleaned using acetone and water, and irradiated with UV light in a vacuum (H0017, Japan) for 5 min. ZnO NWs (50 mg), ZnO NPs (50 mg), ZnO NWs@C-dots (50 mg), and ZnO NPs@C-dots (50 mg) were dispersed and stirred for 4 h in 400 μL of water separately. The aqueous dispersions of the four samples were coated on 1 × 1 cm2 area of pre-cleaned PEN electrode using a spin coater (K-359SD-1 SPINNER, KYOWA RIKEN, Japan) at 2000 rpm and dried separately.

The four sample electrodes annealed in an oven at 80 °C for 30 min were immersed for 12 h in ethanol (5 mL) solution of N719 (0.5 mM) dyes, separately. The four samples photo-cathode films (0.142 mm thickness) on PEN electrode were assembled with the platinum (Pt)/PEN counter electrode. Redox electrolytes (0.3 M KI, 0.05 M I2, and 10 mL of acetonitrile solvent) were added between two constructed film electrodes on PEN. The I–V measurement was performed utilizing a blue (430 nm) light-emitting diode (LED) light source with a ZAHNER, Xpot, 26356 German, instrument. The calculation of total PCE (
η
) at the intensity of light incident
(
P in
= 100
W
m 2
)
follows the equation below.
(1)
η =
J sc
V oc
P in
F F
where
P in
is light incident power, J sc is the circuit current density, V oc is the open-circuit voltage and FF is the fill factor.

As shown in Fig. 1 a, the morphology of the prepared zinc oxide nanowires (ZnO) was determined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Average ZnO NW length and diameter were 1–1.5 μm and 30–40 nm, respectively. An essential solution component for the development and growth of ZnO NWs is sodium carbonate. 22 As exhibited in Fig. 1b, ZnO NPs were shaped like polygons. Fig. 1c displays the size distributions determined by sampling the particles in the TEM images. Accordingly, 20 the average particle size of ZnO NPs yielded by the polyol solvent technique was 23.5 ± 9.8 nm.

The UV–visible absorption spectra (Fig. 2 a) for ZnO NWs, ZnO NPs, ZnO NWs@C-dots, C-dots (12 wt%), and ZnO NPs@C-dots all revealed one band at different wavelengths. As a result, C-dot contributes to the ZnO NWs and ZnO NPs bands at 357 nm and 392 nm respectively. When C-dots are applied onto zinc oxide nanowires (ZnO NWs) and zinc oxide nanoparticles (ZnO NPs), there is a noticeable shift towards longer wavelengths, resulting in red shifts in the absorption bands previously observed at 357 and 392 nm. 23 The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of C-dots and ZnO NPs@C-dots are displayed in Fig. 2b. Two excitation bands of C-dots and ZnO NPs@C-dots at 245 and 343 nm, and a corresponding emission band at 438 nm were presented. ZnO NPs@C-dots had two excitation bands at 243 and 374 nm, and their corresponding emission band was at 442 nm. 24

Fig. 3 displays the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of C-dots, ZnO NPs, ZnO NWs, and ZnO NPs@C-dots. The stretching vibration modes of ZnO NWs and ZnO NPs can be attributed to the main bands appearing at 510 cm−1 and 471 cm−1 respectively. FT-IR spectra (Table 1 ) of ZnO NWs and ZnO NPs showed bands of O–H stretching (both at 3434 cm−1), O–H bending (both at 1381 cm−1), and Zn–O stretching (510 and 471 cm−1). When the O–H stretching (3434 cm−1) of ZnO NW and O–H bending (1381 cm−1) of ZnO NPs can be assigned to the O–H bending modes' contribution, which should have originated from the water adsorption or hydration or hydroxylation on the surfaces of ZnO NWs and ZnO NPs respectively. Five significant bands of C-dots are likely seen at 3403, 2930, 1661, 1556, and 1389 cm−1. They are attributed to the respective vibration modes of carboxylic acid (O–H, CC), alkyl (C–H), amine (N–H), and graphitic (CC) functional groups. ZnO NPs@C-dots, on the other hand, exhibit distinctive bands of both ZnO NPs and C-dots, 25
,
26 demonstrating the coexistence of both elements. Therefore, C-dots and ZnO NPs coexist from FT-IR spectra.

Fig. 4 displays the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of ZnO NWs, ZnO NPs, ZnO NWs@C-dots, and ZnO NPs@C-dots. Peaks at 2θ around 31.70°, 34.40°, 36.20°, 47.50°, 56.50°, 62.80°, 67.90°, and 69.00° are indicated by the XRD pattern of ZnO NWs and correspond to the lattice planes of ZnO NWs with space group P63 mc (186) for the indices (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103), (200), (112), and (201), respectively (JCPDF file no.89-0510). The lattice parameter values for ZnO NWs were a = b = 3.24880, c = 5.20540, and c/a ratio = 1.60225. This material has a hexagonal wurtzite structure. 27 Similarly, the XRD diffraction peaks values of ZnO NPs provide the same indices information at 2θ = 31.73°, 34.42°, 36.26°, 47.53°, 56.53°, 62.87°, 66.35°, 67.86°, and 69.04°, respectively. The values of the lattice parameter were a = b = 3.25300, c = 5.21300, and c/a ratio = 1.60252, which corresponds to a hexagonal wurtzite structure. 20 The composites of ZnO NWs@C-dots and ZnO NPs@C-dots showed lattice plane and hexagonal wurtzite structure peaks similar to the XRD peaks of ZnO NWs and ZnO NPs. Therefore, the C-dots most probably have amorphous state, and the crystallinity and morphology of ZnO NWs and ZnO NPs are not affected by C-dot addition.

The isotherms of nitrogen desorption at 77 K for powders of ZnO NPs and ZnO NPs@C-dots were measured. Fig. 5 and Table 2 display the isotherms and BET parameters, respectively. When C-dots were deposited onto ZnO NPs, the specific surface area of the composite material (ZnO NPs@C-dots) increased to 64.5 m2 g−1. This represents a three-fold increase in the surface area compared to ZnO NPs alone.

The enhanced specific surface area of ZnO NPs@C-dots suggests that the smaller particles of C-dots with their large specific surface area contribute to the increased overall surface area of the composite material. This result indicates that introducing C-dots onto ZnO NPs leads to a significant increase in the available surface area for chemical reactions or interactions, which can be beneficial for various applications such as catalysis or energy conversion.

In summary, the introduction of C-dots onto ZnO NPs increases the specific surface area of the ZnO NPs from 20.4 m2 g−1 to 64.5 m2 g−1, which can have important implications for their properties and potential applications.

Although the mean pore width was reduced following the binding of ZnO NPs, the specific surface area and pore volume of ZnO NPs@C-dots were larger than those of ZnO NPs. In a similar vein, the binding of C-dots increased the specific surface area and pore volume of ZnO NPs@C-dots relative to ZnO NPs. 28

The absorption edge for transitions can be obtained because of the model proposed by Tauc 29
,
30 (Fig. 6 ) as follows:
(2)
α h ν = A
(
h ν −
E g
)
n
Where
A
is an energy-independent constant related to the properties of the valence and conduction bands,
h ν
is the photon energy,
α
is the absorption coefficient, and
E g
is the optical energy band gap of the material. The value of parameter ‘
n
’ for allowed direct transition is
½
. 30

The absorption edge is well-fitted by the change of (αhν) 2 against (hν), and the extrapolated (hν) at (αhν) 2 = 0 provides the optical band gap. For instance, Fig. 6 illustrates the fluctuation of (αhν) 2 against (hν) and demonstrates how the intercepts of extrapolations to zero with the photon energy axis (αhν) 2
→
0 were used to calculate the optical energy gap. According to the results, the bandgap values for ZnO NWs, ZnO NPs, ZnO NWs@C-dots, and ZnO NPs@C-dots photo anodes are 3.26, 3.24, 3.20, and 3.13 eV respectively. As a result, Table 3 contains an evaluation of the bandgap energies. Thus, as many researchers have clearly shown, narrower band gaps can increase charge transfer in solar cells.

The electrochemical parameters of the ZnO NWs, ZnO NPs, ZnO NWs@C-dots, and ZnO NPs@C-dots electrodes employed as a substrate for PET flexible DSSCs were examined. Their power conversation efficiencies were (1.45 ± 0.03)%, (2.34 ± 0.04)%, (4.251 ± 0.02)% and (5.81 ± 0.023)%, respectively (Table 3). C-dots are anticipated to be easily incorporated into ZnO NPs due to the electrostatic attraction force, which can also boost the specific surface area and visible spectrum light absorption of ZnO NPs@C-dots composite.

The impact of C-dots on FDSSC (flexible dye-sensitized solar cell) performance was previously assessed and showed that it effectively produces a quick photocurrent response as reported by Etefa et al. 25 As seen in Fig. 7 and Table 3, by loading the optimized C-dots (with CA : EDA molar ratio of 1 : 1.5) at 12.5 wt% deposited on the substrate, the current density was enhanced from 1.63 ± 0.13 mA cm−2 to 2.760 ± 0.21 mA cm−2, and as a result, its PCE was increased from (1.45 ± 0.03)% to (5.81 ± 0.023)%. The augmentation of charge separation, decrease of charge recombination, and therefore a boost in charge transport at ZnO NPs are caused by the interaction between C-dots and ZnO NPs, and these effects are crucial for photocurrent production.

In general, DSSCs have low PCEs in comparison to other solar cells, and nanowire semiconductor based DSSCs have particularly low PCEs compared to nanoparticles semiconductors DSSCs. In contrast to metal compound-based quantum dot solar cells, the PCE of C-quantum dot solar cells is likewise quite low. However, the current study has demonstrated that the coexistence of two photosensitizers, dye and C-dots, significantly improved the efficiency of the nanoparticles semiconductors DSSCs.

According to predictions made from the absorption of dye in Table 3, the differences between ZnO NPs-based solar cells and ZnO NWs-based solar cells should result from the different sensitizing mechanisms of C-dots and N719. As seen in Fig. 7a, the best performance of ZnO NPs achieved at the constrained content of C-dots (12.5 wt%) was 5.81%. While ZnO NWs with C-dots achieved 4.251%, which is lower than that of ZnO NPs with C-dots. This means that among different amounts of C-dots tested, ZnO NPs showed their highest performance when a specific amount (12.5 wt%) of C-dots was added. Since the adsorption amount of dye is very convenient at 0.381 mg/mg, the potency of C-dots can be considered as the contribution to the electron-hole pair separation in conducting nanoparticles in various ways from photosensitizer. This suggests that when a higher amount of C-dots is added, there is excess adsorption (binding) of the C-dots on the ZnO NPs. This excess adsorption affects the separation of electron-hole pairs (generated by the photosensitizer) in the conducting nanoparticles, which is essential for efficient solar cell performance.

Table 4 shows the findings of recent works on quantum dots and N719 co-sensitized DSSC. Two other groups Getachew and Etefa presented data on ZnO NWs/C-dots/TOCNF DSSCs, while C. Bonilha and H. Abdullah presented data on ZnO NWs/C-dots/TOCNF and StSta/TNPsITO/PET. Despite N719 being more additive on a ZnO NWs/C-dots/TOCNF working electrode than ZnO NPs/C-dots working electrode, the enhancement effect of quantum dots on their case-based DSSCs was not obvious (with efficiency increased only from 1.31% to 1.34%).

In the provided Table 4, the performance of various flexible electrodes for DSSCs is compared. The table includes information about the materials used, working electrode (WE), counter electrode (CE), deposition methods, power conversion efficiency (PCE) in percentage, and corresponding references. It is mentioned that the addition of C-dots to ZnO NWs and ZnO NPs resulted in a significant enhancement in the performance of DSSCs. The ZnO NWs@C-dots DSSC showed an approximate 2 times improvement, while the ZnO NPs@C-dots DSSC demonstrated a 3 times enhancement. Additionally, there was a synergistic interaction between C-dots, N719 dye, and ZnO NPs and ZnO NWs solar cells, indicating that the combination of C-dots and N719 dye had a positive effect on the performance of the DSSCs. It has been demonstrated that C-dots are outstanding electron donors and/or receivers. This result clearly demonstrates the superb dual functionality of photo-excited C-dots as an electron donor and acceptor. C-dots are well known for having exceptional photo-induced electron transfer and light harvesting capabilities, which enable their photocatalytic and light-energy conversion capabilities. By enhancing dye adsorption and therefore improving photocurrent generation, C-dots lead to a better light harvest and efficient PCE. They also result in higher mobilities and a reduction in recombination. Lastly, C-dots lead to promising photogenerated charge separation.

The performance of DSSCs made of two different materials ZnO NWs and ZnO NPs was investigated in this work with the goal of enhancing it through the addition of C-dots. ZnO NPs@C-dots with a smaller band gap (3.13 eV) showed stronger sensitizer adsorption ability and higher performance than ZnO NWs DSSCs because they had a larger surface area per volume. The rise in short current density and consequently the efficiency of PCE were impacted by this phenomenon. The performance of the photovoltaic system was also improved by the addition of C-dots to ZnO NPs. Nevertheless, the best performance of ZnO NPs achieved at the constrained content of C-dots (12.5 wt%) was 5.81%. Because of the excess adsorption, the potency of C-dots contributes to the electron-hole pair separation in conducting nanoparticles in various ways from a photosensitizer. The current analysis verified that the conversion efficiencies of ZnO NWs@C-dots/N719 and ZnO NPs@C-dots/N719 DSSCs were 4.25% and 5.81%, respectively, at a 1 : 1.5 M ratio of CA/EDA in C-dots. This experiment used low-cost additives but high-effective contributions (C-dots) to improve the effectiveness of DSSCs.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests which may be considered as potential competing interests: the research project is funded by ESKOM.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The writers would like to express their sincere gratitude to Walter Sisulu University (WSU) and the ESKOM for financial support.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: ZnO nanoparticles based dye sensitized solar cell: fabrication and characterization
AIP Conference Proceedings

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Agrawal, A.; Siddiqui, S. A.; Soni, A.; Sharma, G.; Shrivastava, D. R. ZnO nanoparticles based dye sensitized solar cell: Fabrication and characterization. In AIP Conference Proceedings, 2020; AIP Publishing: Vol. 2294.

Effect of TiO2 particle and pore size on DSSC efficiency. Materials for Renewable and Sustainable Energy 2020, 9, 1-8.

Mater Technol

J Sol Gel Sci Technol

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Habtamu Fekadu Etefa is currently a postdoctoral researcher supervising 3 honor MSc students at Walter Sisulu University, South Africa. He has supervised 5 postgraduates, and 1 co-supervised PhD student, and published more than 13 research papers, and three book chapters. He is an active guest editor of 1 journal, awarded a review certificate, delivered 5 invited talks, evaluated 15 external MSc theses and presented 15 conference papers. He has been awarded as an international conference organizing convener. He has worked on an e-International Organizing Committee, as a reviewer guest editor, and editor. His research interests are preparation, characterization, application for energy conversion, photocatalytic, solar energy, and hydrogen evolusion.
